lessons.title: Syntax: concept, requires, Standard Concepts

background.label: C++17: SFINAE and enable_if
background.body: Before C++20, constraints on template types were expressed via SFINAE: std::enable_if_t<condition, T> in the signature caused a substitution failure when the condition was violated. This worked, but produced unreadable compiler errors and required deep knowledge of SFINAE idioms. Concepts solve the same problem declaratively and with understandable diagnostics.

lessons.motivation: Imagine a template function that can be called with any type — but is meaningful only for types that support addition. Before C++20, the only way to reject 'wrong' types was SFINAE: std::enable_if_t in the return type or in a dummy template parameter. On error, the compiler would produce 40 lines of unreadable substitution failures. Concepts let you write a constraint once, give it a name, and get a human-readable error message — the compiler explicitly names the violated concept and the specific requirement that failed.
lessons.theory:
A concept is a compile-time predicate on types (or values). It is declared with the concept keyword and evaluates to true or false when a concrete type is substituted. If a concept is violated, substitution is rejected with a clear message: the compiler names the concept and the requirement that failed — unlike SFINAE, where the error chain could span dozens of lines.

The core of the syntax is the requires-expression: requires(parameters) { requirements; }. Four kinds of requirements are allowed inside. A simple requirement is an arbitrary expression that must be syntactically valid: c.begin(); checks that the begin method exists. A compound requirement wraps the expression in braces and specifies the expected result type after ->: { c.size() } -> std::convertible_to<std::size_t>; requires that size() returns something convertible to size_t. A type requirement checks for a nested type's existence: typename T::value_type;. A nested requirement refers to another concept: requires std::copyable<T>;.

A concept is applied to a template in three equivalent syntaxes. The first — in the template parameter list: template<Addable T> — where the concept name replaces typename. The second — abbreviated function template syntax: T add(Addable auto a, Addable auto b) — each auto parameter is checked against the concept independently. The third — trailing requires-clause: template<typename T> T add(T a, T b) requires Addable<T> — convenient when the constraint depends on multiple parameters or needs a compound condition (Addable<T> && std::copyable<T>).

The standard library ships ready-made concepts in the <concepts> header. Type categories: std::integral<T>, std::floating_point<T>, std::signed_integral<T>, std::unsigned_integral<T>. Type relationships: std::same_as<T,U>, std::derived_from<D,B>, std::convertible_to<From,To>. Object concepts: std::copyable<T>, std::movable<T>, std::default_initializable<T>, std::destructible<T>. Comparison: std::equality_comparable<T>, std::totally_ordered<T>, std::regular<T> (= copyable + equality_comparable + default_initializable). Callable: std::invocable<F,Args...>, std::predicate<F,Args...>. Using standard concepts avoids writing your own for common cases and guarantees correct semantics.

examples.title: A custom concept and checking it
examples.expectedOutput:
item: (3, 4)
Point satisfies Printable: true
Opaque satisfies Printable: false
examples.explanation: The Printable concept is a compound requirement: the to_string() method must exist and return something convertible to std::string. Point satisfies the concept, Opaque does not. A concept's value for a concrete type can be checked directly in code: Printable<Point> evaluates to true or false at compile time, and std::boolalpha prints it as 'true'/'false' instead of 1/0.

examples.title: Standard concepts: integral, floating_point, totally_ordered
examples.expectedOutput:
double_it(5): 10
double_it(2.5): 5
clamp(15, 0, 10): 10
clamp(-3, 0, 10): 0
int is integral: true
double is integral: false
int is regular: true
examples.explanation: Two overloaded double_it functions: one accepts std::integral, the other std::floating_point. Overload resolution uses concepts: a call with int goes to the first, with double to the second. clamp is constrained by std::totally_ordered — the type must support < and > operators. The concept std::regular combines copyable + equality_comparable + default_initializable; int satisfies all three.

examples.title: All four kinds of requirements in a requires-expression
examples.expectedOutput:
size: 3, empty: false
vector<int> is Container: true
int is Container: false
string is Container: true
examples.explanation: The Container concept demonstrates all four requirement kinds in one place. Simple requirements c.begin() and c.end() just check expression validity. Compound requirements qualify the result type: size() must be convertible to size_t, empty() must return exactly bool. The type requirement typename T::value_type checks for a nested type. The nested requirement requires std::copyable<typename T::value_type> verifies that the container's elements are copyable. std::string satisfies the concept because it has iterators, size(), empty(), value_type (= char), and char is copyable.

lessons.title: Constrained Templates and Abbreviated Function Templates
lessons.motivation: Concepts from the previous lesson describe requirements on types — but how exactly do you attach a concept to a template? C++20 offers four distinct syntactic forms, each convenient in a different situation. On top of that, the new abbreviated function template syntax lets you write constrained templates without the template keyword at all — just `std::integral auto x` in the function parameter. Understanding all four forms and their nuances (in particular, that each `auto` introduces a separate independent type parameter) prevents hard-to-spot bugs and makes template interfaces readable at a glance.
lessons.theory:
C++20 provides four equivalent syntaxes for applying a concept to a template. First: the concept name in place of typename in the parameter list — `template<std::integral T>`. Second: abbreviated function template syntax — `void f(std::integral auto x)`, where the compiler implicitly introduces a template parameter. Third: a trailing requires-clause after the signature — `template<typename T> void f(T x) requires std::integral<T>`. Fourth: a requires-clause in the template head — `template<typename T> requires std::integral<T> void f(T x)`. All four produce identical code; the choice is a matter of readability: the first is concise for a single parameter, the third and fourth are convenient for compound conditions like `Concept1<T> && Concept2<T>`.

Abbreviated function templates (`concept auto`) are a key convenience in C++20. Writing `void print_sum(std::integral auto a, std::integral auto b)` is equivalent to `template<std::integral T, std::integral U> void print_sum(T a, U b)`: each `auto` introduces a separate independent template parameter. This is an important distinction from the explicit `template<std::integral T> void print_sum(T a, T b)`, where both parameters must be the same type. If you need two arguments to share the same type, use the explicit form with a single named parameter `T`.

Concepts can be applied not only to template functions but also to individual methods of a class template. Writing `T max() const requires std::totally_ordered<T>` makes the method simply not exist for types that do not satisfy the concept — attempting to call it yields a clear compiler error, but instantiating the class for other types is unaffected. This lets you build classes with conditional capabilities without specializations or SFINAE.

Non-type template parameters (NTTPs) also support concepts: `template<std::integral auto N>` accepts only integral constants. Similarly, `concept auto` works in variable declarations: `std::integral auto n = 42;` compiles, while `std::integral auto x = 3.14;` does not. Such declarations document intent and catch assignment errors at the point they occur rather than deeper in the code.

examples.title: All four constraint syntaxes and abbreviated templates
examples.explanation: All four functions are constrained by the same std::integral concept but written differently. square1 uses the concept directly in the template parameter — the most concise form. add demonstrates the abbreviated syntax: `add(3, 4L)` works because int and long are two separate template parameters, each checked independently. square3 and square4 use a requires-clause; the only difference is its position — after or before the signature. All four produce identical code.

examples.title: Constrained class method and NTTP with concept
examples.explanation: SortedBuffer<T> keeps elements in sorted order after each insertion. The min() and max() methods are marked `requires std::totally_ordered<T>` — for types without an operator< they simply do not exist: the compiler will not reject creating a SortedBuffer<SomeUnsortableType> unless you actually try to call those methods. RingSize demonstrates constraining a non-type template parameter: `requires (N > 0)` guarantees a positive ring buffer size at compile time.

lessons.title: Subsumption, Conjunction, Disjunction and Concept-Based Overloading
lessons.motivation: Concepts don't just express constraints — they change the rules of overload resolution. With multiple overloads constrained by different concepts, a C++20 compiler can automatically pick the 'most specific' one without help from enable_if priority tricks or tag types. The mechanism is called subsumption: concept A subsumes concept B if A's constraints logically include B's. Understanding subsumption and how `&&` and `||` in concepts affect overload ordering lets you write clean multi-level APIs — no hacks, no SFINAE ladders, with readable compiler errors when a constraint is violated.
lessons.theory:
Conjunction (`&&`) and disjunction (`||`) in concept definitions work exactly as intuition suggests. `template<typename T> concept SignedIntegral = std::integral<T> && std::signed_integral<T>;` requires both properties simultaneously. `template<typename T> concept Numeric = std::integral<T> || std::floating_point<T>;` accepts either. The logical operators in concepts do more than compute a boolean result — they participate in constraint normalization, which is the foundation of subsumption.

Subsumption is the 'more constrained' relation that the compiler derives from concept structure. Concept A subsumes concept B if every atomic predicate of B appears (via `&&`/`||` decomposition) in A as well. When two overloads are both applicable — one constrained by B and one by A — the compiler selects the A overload as 'more specialized'. No extra annotation is needed: `std::signed_integral<T>` is standardly defined in terms of `std::integral<T> && ...`, so a signed_integral-constrained overload automatically wins over an integral-constrained one for signed types. For an unsigned type, signed_integral fails, and the integral overload wins.

A critical limitation: subsumption only works through named concepts, not raw expressions. If you write `requires (sizeof(T) > 1)` and `requires (sizeof(T) > 1 && std::integral<T>)`, the compiler treats each `sizeof(T) > 1` as a separate opaque atomic constraint — even if they are textually identical. Subsumption does not apply, and a call that satisfies both overloads results in an ambiguity error. For subsumption to kick in, the shared part of the constraint must be extracted into a named concept so the compiler sees the same name on both sides and can establish the 'more constrained' relation.

Concepts replace SFINAE in overloading not just technically but semantically. Instead of `template<typename T, std::enable_if_t<std::is_integral_v<T>, int> = 0> void f(T x);` you write `template<std::integral T> void f(T x);`. Both SFINAE-discard unsuitable types, but the concepts version: participates in subsumption (so a more specific overload can 'win' over it), supports abbreviated syntax (`void f(std::integral auto x)`), and on constraint violation produces a message like 'constraints not satisfied: T = std::string, std::integral<T> requires integer type' — instead of a multi-line SFINAE error dump.

examples.title: Subsumption: three levels of specialization
examples.expectedOutput:
classify(42):             signed integral (multi-byte)
classify(42u):            integral
classify((short)1):       signed integral (multi-byte)
classify((signed char)1): signed integral
examples.explanation: The three classify overloads form a subsumption hierarchy. `std::signed_integral<T>` is defined in terms of `std::integral<T>`, so the second overload is 'more constrained' than the first. The third adds `sizeof(T) > 1` via `&&` — making it 'more constrained' than the second. For `int` (signed, 4 bytes) the third wins. For `unsigned int` only `std::integral` holds — the first wins. For `short` (signed, 2 bytes) the third wins again. For `signed char` (signed, 1 byte) `sizeof(T) > 1` is false — the second wins. The compiler resolves all of this without any extra hints.

examples.title: Conjunction, disjunction and replacing SFINAE
examples.expectedOutput:
wide integral store: 42 (4 bytes)
wide integral store: 42 (8 bytes)
other numeric store: 3.14
other numeric store: 1
other numeric store: 2.718
WideIntegral<int>:    true
WideIntegral<short>:  false
Numeric<double>:      true
Numeric<std::string>: false
examples.explanation: WideIntegral is a conjunction: the type must be integral (`std::integral<T>`) and at least 4 bytes wide. Numeric is a disjunction: being either integral or floating-point is sufficient. For `short`, WideIntegral is false (sizeof==2), so `store((short)1)` routes to the second overload and prints `1`. The second overload explicitly excludes WideIntegral via `requires (!WideIntegral<T>)` — the standard way to partition overloads when no automatic subsumption exists between the concepts. Concept values (`WideIntegral<int>` etc.) can be checked directly in code — they evaluate to `true`/`false` at compile time.

lessons.title: Advanced Patterns: Concepts in Classes and Ranges Concepts
